skillName: Write positive integers in the form A × 10ⁿ
misconceptionSummary: Students write A outside the range 1 ≤ A < 10, leaving two or more digits before the decimal point.

lines.text: A = 36 — two digits before decimal point

steps.narration: The student correctly found that the significant digits are three and six, and that the remaining part is ten to the four. But A must be between one and ten — thirty-six breaks that rule.

lines.text: Multiply the power by 10 to compensate: 10⁴ × 10 = 10⁵

steps.narration: Divide A by ten to bring it into range: thirty-six becomes three point six. To keep the overall value the same, multiply the power of ten by ten — increasing the exponent by one.
Three point six times ten to the five. Verify by expanding: three point six times one hundred thousand is three hundred and sixty thousand. Correct.

visuals.label: A too large → divide A by 10, add 1 to exponent

steps.narration: Three point six times ten to the five. One digit before the decimal in A — standard form is correct.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 36 × 10⁴ where A has two digits. Fix it: divide A by 10 and add 1 to the exponent → 3.6 × 10⁵. Always check that 1 ≤ A < 10.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: If A is 10 or more, divide it by 10 and increase the exponent by 1. Keep adjusting until A is between 1 and 10.
